title: Multitime memory beyond the quantum regression theorem in sequential measurement statistics
authors: Paolo Luppi, Claudia Benedetti, Andrea Smirne
abstract: We investigate the presence of memory in the sequential measurement statistics of an open quantum system, as witnessed by the departure from the quantum regression theorem (QRT), that is, the possibility to predict multitime probabilities from the one-time reduced dynamical map. For factorized initial states, we identify an exact decomposition of the two-time propagator into a QRT-like contribution, fully determined by the reduced dynamical map, and a memory term encoding system--environment correlations across the intervention; in the weak-coupling regime, the memory term yields an explicit second-order correction expressed in terms of the reduced map and bath correlation functions. Furthermore, we introduce an operational quantifier of QRT violations based on the distance between exact and QRT-predicted joint probabilities. Benchmarking the framework on a spin--boson model and using a pseudomode embedding as nonperturbative reference, we comprehensively analyze the impact of spectral-density parameters, environmental temperature, and measurement protocols on the non-Markovianity of the multitime statistics. Comparison with a one-time quantifier shows that reduced-state non-Markovianity and multitime memory are related but inequivalent: the latter, as probed through sequential statistics, is intrinsically protocol dependent and can become visible at higher temporal order even when two-time statistics remain compatible with QRT predictions.
summary: This paper investigates how the Quantum Regression Theorem fails in sequential measurements due to system-environment correlations. It introduces a way to quantify this 'multitime memory' and shows that higher-order measurement protocols can reveal non-Markovian effects that are invisible in simpler two-time statistics.
main_problem: Investigating the breakdown of the Quantum Regression Theorem (QRT) in sequential measurement statistics and distinguishing between one-time non-Markovianity and multitime memory.
main_result: The authors show that multitime memory is protocol-dependent and can manifest at higher temporal orders even when two-time statistics obey the QRT; they also derive a second-order correction for the weak-coupling regime.
method: The study uses projection-operator techniques to decompose the two-time propagator, a perturbative expansion for weak coupling, and pseudomode embedding for non-perturbative benchmarking.
model_or_system: An open quantum system (specifically a spin-boson model/qubit) coupled to a Gaussian bosonic bath with a Lorentzian spectral density.
key_observables: Two-time and three-time joint probabilities, two-time correlators, and an operational quantifier of QRT violation based on Kolmogorov distance.
important_parameters: Coupling strength (lambda), spectral density parameters (center eta and width gamma), environmental temperature (beta), and measurement protocols (commuting vs. non-commuting).
main_assumptions: The system starts in a factorized initial state with a Gaussian bath, and the derived correction is specifically valid in the weak-coupling regime.
figures_summary: Figures compare one-time vs. two-time non-Markovianity, show the reduction of error using the second-order correction, illustrate temperature dependence, and demonstrate how higher-order (three-time) protocols reveal more memory than two-time protocols.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the theoretical derivation of the propagator decomposition and the weak-coupling correction to numerical benchmarking using the spin-boson model, followed by a comprehensive analysis of the impact of temperature, spectral density, and measurement protocols.
why_interesting: It provides a new framework for understanding how system-environment correlations are revealed through sequential measurements, which is crucial for interpreting experimental data in open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Two-time landscape of QRT violation. Heatmap of the QRT-violation quantifier εQRT(t2, t1) defined in Eq.(28 over the (t1, t2) plane, for γ = 0.1, ω0 = η = 4.5, λ = 0.1 [here and in the follow- ing all parameters are in arbitrary units], ρS (0) = |0⟩⟨0| and a zero- temperature environment; the pseudomode levels are cut at nmax = 8. The white triangular region corresponds to t2 < t1.

Figure 2. Average two-time non-Markovianity versus average one-time non-Markovianity. (Left) ¯εQRT(tf ) defined in Eq.(30) over the (ω0, γ) plane, for the sequential-measurement protocol with ρS (0) = |0⟩⟨0| and projective σz measurements at t1 and t f = 10; η = 4.5, λ = 0.1, nmax = 10 and zero-temperature environment. (Central) ¯N(Λ; tf ) defined in Eq.(55) for the corresponding one-time reduced dynamics. (Right) Same quantity and values as the central panel, but for tf = 30.

Figure 4 shows ¯εQRT for representative inverse temperatures β, and hence thermal occupations nβ. Across all cases, the region where QRT deviations are appreciable remains local- ized in the long-memory regime (small γ) and is typically en- hanced near resonance ω0 ≃η. Increasing the temperature leads to a quantitative enhancement of the average QRT vio- lation in the same long-memory region, indicating that ther- mal fluctuations amplify the two-time memory for the present spin–boson setting. This behavior can be understood in terms of the P −Q decomposition introduced in Sec. III A. At finite temperature the pseudomode is thermally populated, which increases the number of pathways through...

Figure 3. Second-order correction beyond-QRT. QRT violation εQRT(t2, t1) as defined in Eq.(28) and residual error after including the second-order correction, ελ2(t2, t1) [Eq.(57)], as a function of the coupling strength λ. Parameters: γ = 0.1, ω0 = η = 4.5, zero- temperature environment and nmax = 8. Protocol: ρS (0) = |+⟩⟨+|; L¨uders projective measurement in the σz basis at t1 = 5 and readout in the σx basis at t2 = 10.

Figure 4. Temperature dependence of the average QRT violation. ¯εQRT(tf ) as defined in Eq.(30) over the (ω0, γ) plane at fixed bath peak η = 4.5 and coupling λ = 0.1, using the sequential measurement protocol consisting of ρS (0) = |0⟩⟨0| and projective σz measurements with L¨uders update. The three panels refer to different environmental temperatures: zero temperature (left panel), β = 0.25 (central panel), corresponding to a mean occupation number nβ = 0.48, and β = 0.154 (right panel), corresponding to a mean occupation number nβ = 1.002; the pseudomode has been correspondingly truncated at different values of the maximum level nmax: 8 (left), 13 (center) and 20 (right).
